title: Golf Shoe Care Mistakes That Ruin Leather and Mesh
description: Most damage to golf shoes doesn't come from the course. It comes from how they're cleaned afterward.

body.text: A round of golf is genuinely hard on shoes — grass, mud, sun, sand. But a lot of the damage golfers blame on "a tough course" actually happens afterward, during cleaning. The wrong product or the wrong technique can do more lasting harm than the round itself.
Mistake 1: Using whatever's under the sink

Dish soap, all-purpose cleaner, glass cleaner — all formulated for surfaces that aren't leather, mesh, or foam. Used occasionally, the damage is minor. Used as a repeated habit, the wrong pH or added chemicals dry out leather and break down glue over a season.
Mistake 2: Scrubbing hard on a stain

It feels productive, but aggressive back-and-forth scrubbing on a fresh stain usually grinds it deeper into mesh and stitching instead of lifting it out. Light, one-directional brushing after the dirt has been loosened works better than force.
Mistake 3: Drying shoes in direct heat

A hot trunk, a sunny windowsill, a heater vent — all speed up drying, and all accelerate the exact damage golfers are trying to prevent. Heat is what actually yellows white leather and stiffens material over time, more than normal wear does.

Fully submerging a golf shoe or hosing it down can force water into the midsole foam and internal seams, where it doesn't dry evenly and can eventually break down the structure. Spot-cleaning the dirty areas gets the same result without that risk.

Letting dirt and grass dry and set over days means every clean starts from a harder position, which means more scrubbing, which means more wear. The single easiest way to avoid all of the above is cleaning a little, right after the round, before anything has time to set.

G-SCRUB's kit is built around avoiding exactly these mistakes — a pH-conscious foam formula, a brush designed for one-directional cleaning, and a routine quick enough to do right after the round instead of days later.
